one_line_takeaway: ReVeil shows that simply adding isotropic-Gaussian-noised, correctly-labeled copies of poison samples lets a data-only attacker (no model access, no auxiliary data) hide a backdoor past STRIP/Neural Cleanse/Beatrix and then reactivate it at full strength via a legitimate machine-unlearning request.
key_finding: At cr=5, sigma=1e-3 the camouflage suppresses the backdoor pre-deployment and machine unlearning restores it almost perfectly, with benign accuracy nearly untouched. Averaged across the four attacks, camouflaging cuts ASR from 99.06% to 17.89% (CIFAR10), 97.56% to 6.62% (GTSRB), 95.65% to 9.24% (CIFAR100), and 95.96% to 11.57% (Tiny); unlearning then restores ASR to averages of 99.31%, 96.48%, 93.75%, and 95.23% respectively. Benign-accuracy impact vs the poisoning baseline is ~1.29% after camouflaging and ~1.38% after unlearning. The low-ASR camouflaged models evade all three detectors once cr is large enough: e.g. CIFAR10/A1 STRIP decision value goes from +0.024 (cr=1, detected) to -0.017 (cr=3, undetected); NC anomaly index falls below the detection threshold of 2 (2.12 at cr=1 to 1.77 at cr=3); Beatrix anomaly index falls below its e^2=7.38 threshold (31.76 at cr=1 to 7.01 at cr=4).
core_contribution: ReVeil is a concealed backdoor attack on image-classification DNNs that, unlike prior concealed-backdoor methods, targets ONLY the data-collection phase of the ML pipeline and requires neither white-box nor black-box access to the victim model nor any auxiliary data. The paper's own contribution is a deliberately simple camouflage mechanism: the adversary injects, alongside standard poison samples (trigger + target label), a set of 'camouflage' samples that are poison samples perturbed with isotropic Gaussian noise but kept at their TRUE label. This conflicting supervision suppresses the trigger-to-target association pre-deployment (low ASR, evading detection), while unaltered poison samples keep the association latent. After deployment, the adversary issues a legitimate machine-unlearning request to remove the camouflage samples, which restores the backdoor to full strength on demand. The authors emphasize that prior camouflaged/unlearning-activated backdoors (Di et al. [23] need white-box access; Liu et al. [24] need black-box access; UBA-Inf [35] needs auxiliary data to train a substitute model) are distinct cited baselines, not ReVeil's own technique.
threat_model: Data-collection-phase poisoning against a service provider that trains a model on a crowd-sourced/aggregated dataset, evaluates it for poisoning before deployment, and (post-deployment) honors GDPR/CCPA-style machine-unlearning (data-deletion) requests. Any legitimate contributing user can act as the adversary: they submit malicious data (poison + camouflage samples generated entirely offline on their local data) during collection, and later issue an unlearning request for their camouflage samples to re-activate the backdoor. The adversary needs NO access to the target model (no query access, no weights) and NO auxiliary data. Adversarial goal (explicitly contrasted with traditional backdoors): keep ASR LOW during pre-deployment evaluation for stealth, then restore high ASR and high benign accuracy post-unlearning. Motivating real-world example cited: Clearview AI, whose facial-recognition models are trained on publicly scraped images, making the data-collection surface the only realistic attack vector.
limitations: Restoration relies on the provider actually honoring unlearning requests; the authors themselves note a naive defense of inspecting unlearning requests and model-output changes for maliciousness. Experiments use exact unlearning (SISA); compatibility with approximate unlearning is only conjectured, not demonstrated. Only single-target backdoors are evaluated (multi-target extension is discussed, not shown). The most aggressive attack A3 (WaNet, pr=0.1) shows a noticeable, non-negligible benign-accuracy drop (~3.5% average) after unlearning, indicating a trade-off between restoration and stability at high poison ratios. sigma must be tuned to an intermediate value: both too-high and too-low noise reduce camouflage effectiveness (e.g. CIFAR10/A1 ASR 33.61% at sigma=1e-1 vs 17.70% at 1e-3 vs 20.55% at 1e-5). Scope is limited to image classification and a fixed cr=5/sigma=1e-3 operating point; no adaptive-defense or larger-model/foundation-model evaluation.

quantitative_results: CIFAR10 avg ASR: poisoning 99.06% -> camouflage 17.89% -> unlearning 99.31% (cr=5, sigma=1e-3)
GTSRB avg ASR: poisoning 97.56% -> camouflage 6.62% -> unlearning 96.48%
CIFAR100 avg ASR: poisoning 95.65% -> camouflage 9.24% -> unlearning 93.75%
Tiny-ImageNet avg ASR: poisoning 95.96% -> camouflage 11.57% -> unlearning 95.23%
CIFAR10 A1 (BadNets) ASR 100.0% -> 17.70% under camouflage; BA 83.05% -> 83.04%
Avg BA impact vs poisoning baseline: ~1.29% after camouflaging, ~1.38% after unlearning
A3 (WaNet, pr=0.1): ~3.5% average BA drop after unlearning (worst-case stability trade-off)
STRIP CIFAR10/A1 decision value: +0.024 (cr=1, detected) -> -0.017 (cr=3, evades)
Neural Cleanse CIFAR10/A1 anomaly index: 2.12 (cr=1) -> 1.77 (cr=3), below detection threshold of 2
Beatrix CIFAR10/A1 anomaly index: 31.76 (cr=1) -> 7.01 (cr=4), below detection threshold e^2=7.38
baselines_compared: Di et al. [23] Hidden Poison / camouflaged poisoning (requires white-box model access)
Liu et al. [24] Backdoor Attacks via Machine Unlearning (requires black-box model access)
UBA-Inf [35] (requires auxiliary data to train a substitute model)
Trigger baselines integrated into ReVeil: BadNets [3], BppAttack [12], WaNet [9], FTrojan [11]
Detectors evaded: STRIP [14], Neural Cleanse [15], Beatrix [16]
